title: Can One Talk Wildly, Without Knowing Whether the Mouth or the Mango Is Sour?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The title uses a colloquial Tamil expression for speaking arbitrarily and without factual basis; its imagery and accusatory force are retained. Source comparison confirms that the OCR fragment `snd` is the magazine `கல்கி`, and that the damaged legal phrase concerns a fifty-lakh-rupee damages suit. Other source-visible errors include `இடைத்தோர்தல்`, broken proper names and words, `பரிசீவிப்பதாக`, `மேட்டூர்`, and a missing letter in `நான்`. These are restored only in the derived Tamil reading copy. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: As though the campaigning undertaken by thirty-one ministers and a sixty-one-member election committee in the Yercaud Assembly by-election were not enough, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa left Chennai at 8.30 on the morning of the 28th, flew to Tiruchi, travelled from there by helicopter to Yercaud, and spoke at nine places in about five hours. No, no, she finished reading out the speech prepared for her and returned to Chennai that very evening!

In that speech, she promised a government hospital and government arts college at Vazhapadi; a 3.5-metre tar road for the circumambulatory route around the Thanthondreeswarar temple; primary health centres at M. Perumapalayam, Vellalakundam, Singipuram Panchayat, Ayodhyapattinam and Kootathupatti; veterinary hospitals at Minnampalli, Anuppur, Ayodhyapattinam and A.N. Mangalam; and a sub-health centre in Neyyamalai Panchayat. The newspapers carried her speech.
Tamil Nadu has often witnessed Chief Ministers showering such promises whenever they campaign in by-elections held while the AIADMK is in power.

The lawlessness unleashed by the AIADMK during Tamil Nadu's local-body elections on 16 and 18 October 2001 was no small matter. Because M.K. Stalin contested the office of Mayor of Chennai, the ruling party went to extraordinary lengths to defeat him. Even during the counting of votes, AIADMK members trespassed with police support and created a commotion. Yet, overcoming all these intrigues, younger brother M.K. Stalin won the mayoral contest in Chennai by twelve thousand votes. A candidate who should have won by two lakh votes was declared elected by a margin of merely twelve thousand. In the Madurai Corporation election, DMK candidate and younger brother C. Ramachandran won the mayoralty by about twenty-eight thousand votes. Despite all the lawlessness and violence perpetrated by the ruling party in local-body elections across Tamil Nadu, the party alliance won forty-five municipalities and more than one hundred town panchayats.
In February 2002, ruling-party lawlessness was let loose in the by-elections held in Saidapet, Vaniyambadi and Andipatti. After inspecting the constituency, the election officer stated in his report:

> Mr. Rao returned to the Capital on Sunday night and in his report submitted to the Commission yesterday observed that large scale rigging and malpractices had been done in the revision of the rolls.
Having found irregularities, the Election Commission of India announced the postponement of the by-elections in Saidapet and Vaniyambadi.
Following the Andipatti by-election, elections were held in Vaniyambadi, Saidapet and Acharapakkam on 31 May 2002. AIADMK members conducted them in their customary by-election style.
An editorial in *Kalki* dated 2 February 2003 said this about the Sathankulam by-election:

> Karunanidhi has openly stated that the rulers have already begun work to “delight” the people of Sathankulam. Newspaper reports confirm it. The ruling party knows very well both how to violate the election code of conduct and how to escape being caught by it. The time has come for every non-AIADMK party in Tamil Nadu to set aside its differences and think about the welfare of the state. It must be demonstrated that the people do not like the course of the present AIADMK regime, and that the AIADMK cannot obtain votes even by offering bribes. What benefit or progress has Tamil Nadu seen during this term of AIADMK rule? What achievement has there been other than the withdrawal or dilution of cases against Jayalalithaa? In how many ways have democratic traditions and freedom of the press been disregarded and humiliated? It is important to explain these things and many more, and to defeat the AIADMK. It is even more important to make democracy victorious!

While campaigning in that constituency, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said that a cement factory, a women's college and a polytechnic would be established at Sathankulam. *The Hindu* reported that the ruling party distributed free dhotis, saris, traditional lamps, wristwatches, cricket bats and volleyballs to the constituency's voters. The government immediately filed a fifty-lakh-rupee damages suit against the newspaper and its reporter. After *The Hindu* filed an affidavit in the High Court stating that its reporter had personally toured Sathankulam to gather the news, that everything published was true, and that it possessed supporting evidence, the government ceased pressing the case it had filed.

After tolerating one irregularity after another, the Election Commission drew the Tamil Nadu government's attention to them and sought an explanation from the Chief Secretary. After examining the reply, the Commission sent the state government a letter of censure. It expressed regret that the Chief Secretary's explanation was unsatisfactory. The election rules, it said, prohibited a ruling party from gaining an advantage by making government announcements to win an election, and being in power did not entitle the ruling party to any concession.
The letter continued:

> We regret to state that the Election Commission does not accept the conduct of the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu in announcing new projects during the Sathankulam election campaign. The Election Commission firmly believes that the Chief Minister announced the new projects to gain an electoral advantage. We reject Jayalalithaa's campaign method as unacceptable. The Election Commission concludes that her promises constituted a violation of the election rules.

In his reply to the Chief Election Commissioner's questions about the Sathankulam by-election, Tamil Nadu's Chief Secretary wrote that the Chief Minister had merely said that establishing a cement factory, women's college and sports centre in the constituency would be considered. But the speech published in the ruling party's official newspaper, *Namadhu MGR*, said:

> Immediately after the election, I will extend every assistance to establish both a women's college and a polytechnic at Sathankulam. Vigorous action will be taken to complete the Udangudi-Sathankulam Combined Drinking Water Scheme quickly. A sports centre with all the infrastructure required to realise the ambitions of young people in this constituency will be started here.

Let readers decide whether she said in this speech that these projects would merely “be considered.” Yet the Chief Secretary, a senior IAS officer who should have acted honestly and told the Election Commission the truth, gave false information in his letter. The most painful part is that the Election Commission took no action against him.

Though the Election Commission did not act against the Chief Secretary, the Tamil Nadu government acted swiftly against the District Collector. Because he hesitated to carry out immediately every order issued by ministers in Sathankulam, he was transferred in great haste on the very day after the election result, without being given another posting.

Though the Election Commission took no action against Jayalalithaa, Chief Election Commissioner Lyngdoh told reporters in Delhi that it was regrettable that politicians felt no remorse even after being censured. In an August 2003 interview that revealed the nature of the Jayalalithaa government, he also said:
> The unusually candid Lyngdoh said that the worst States in terms of Electoral Malpractices were U.P., Bihar and Tamil Nadu.
In other words, Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and Tamil Nadu were the three states notorious for electoral malpractice.

It is under an AIADMK government that has earned such praise that the Yercaud by-election is now taking place. Those who continuously watch the news on television and read the newspapers know very well what is happening in that constituency during this by-election.

The main points in Chief Minister Jayalalithaa's speech yesterday were the power cuts and her claim that there was no place even for talk of land grabbing under her government. One can only laugh! On the very morning she said there was no room even for talk of land grabbing, *The Hindu* carried a detailed report that a complaint had been lodged with an Erode police officer about land grabbing by an AIADMK minister and that an inquiry was under way. Only a few months ago, there were detailed reports about land grabbing by a minister from Karur and the case reaching the courts. Yet in Yercaud, the Chief Minister said there was no place even for talk of land grabbing.

On the very day the Chief Minister claimed that people lived peacefully without the nuisance of land grabbing, a seventy-six-year-old Erode resident named Muthusamy tearfully told reporters that his wife had died because people associated with a minister had grabbed his land, and that he had been unable even to attend her funeral.
Speaking at Yercaud, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said: “All power stations under the control of the state government continue, as always, to operate in good condition.” Is that true?
On 28 November 2013, *Dinamalar* reported:

> Tamil Nadu has a state-owned thermal power station in Thoothukudi district. It generates electricity through five units, each with a capacity of 210 megawatts. Power generation in the third unit is frequently affected by boiler-tube punctures. It was therefore decided to stop generation in this unit continuously for thirty days and carry out maintenance. Accordingly, generation was stopped.
Is this the sign that state-controlled power stations are operating in good condition?

On 22 November 2013, *Dinamalar* wrote: “Power generation of about 1,200 megawatts has already been affected in the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board's Mettur expansion and North Chennai expansion units.” Is this the sign that state-controlled power stations are operating in good condition?

The Chief Minister also said that electricity generation had been lost at a thermal station operated by a Union government institution. As an example, she declared in Yercaud: “As though on cue, breakdowns have occurred simultaneously only at the power stations under Union government institutions, including Kalpakkam and Neyveli.”

It is true that generation has fallen at power stations controlled by Union government institutions. But it is not, as the Chief Minister claims, the result of a conspiracy by the Union government. It resembles the saying that to frightened eyes, everything dark looks like a ghost. In the past, whenever there was an accident on the road, she would make the outrageous claim that it was a conspiracy to kill her. This statement is of the same kind.

A Chief Minister must speak responsibly. No government would conspire to reduce electricity generation, and no one would assist such an act. If the Chief Minister makes allegations like these, people will begin to think strangely of her. If a person holding public office were to ask officials to reduce production in public institutions under that person's own control, would those officials comply? Can anyone go to the Union government and ask it to reduce generation? Does none of this strike the Chief Minister as childish?

The reason production fell at Union-controlled power stations was the routine annual maintenance undertaken during the rainy season, when electricity demand is lower. She could have learned this merely by reading the newspapers properly.
On 22 November 2013, *Dinamalar* reported:

> The Union government-owned NLC at Neyveli has three thermal power stations with a combined generating capacity of 2,500 megawatts. Tamil Nadu has been allocated 1,000 megawatts. At present, maintenance is being undertaken in units with a combined generating capacity of 570 megawatts, so only 1,900 megawatts is being generated. Consequently, Tamil Nadu now receives only 750 megawatts from NLC's thermal stations.
Had the Chief Minister read this report, she would have understood the truth. It says clearly that “maintenance is being undertaken.” Production was not reduced through a conspiracy.

The same report categorically stated the previous week: “Power generation at one reactor in the Kalpakkam Atomic Power Station has now been stopped for maintenance.” What will people think when, even after this was stated, a Chief Minister writes that it is all a conspiracy by the Union government?

This maintenance is not something suddenly undertaken now. Every year, power generation is stopped for at least twenty days specifically to carry out maintenance. At least now, let the Chief Minister ask the officials whether this is true and gain some clarity!

The Chief Minister said in Yercaud that “there is a shortage of coal and naphtha needed to generate electricity.” In the Legislative Assembly she said the Union government had not supplied the coal needed by Tamil Nadu's thermal stations. The North Chennai and Mettur stations require ninety lakh tonnes of coal. The Union government has promised to supply only thirty-seven lakh tonnes. It has also allotted Tamil Nadu two mines in northern India from which to extract the coal it needs. Mining is under way in one of them, in Chhattisgarh. But the truth is that the coal shortage arose because the Tamil Nadu government showed insufficient interest in advancing work at the Mandakini mine in Odisha.

After speaking at length about power cuts, the Chief Minister thundered in Yercaud that the Congress and the DMK had joined hands in electricity generation to punish the people of Tamil Nadu. I challenge the Chief Minister: can she prove that I, or anyone from my party, spoke to or contacted anyone in the Union government or any Union government-owned power-generating company about these institutions? Is she ready?
Is it right for someone holding the office of Chief Minister to speak so recklessly, without knowing whether it is the mouth or the mango that has turned sour?
With affection,
M.K.
